rationale_for_baseline_tier: Severe but common interdigital tinea pedis with a mechanical fissure and moccasin plantar involvement in a systemically-well, immunocompetent adult. The patient is afebrile with the source's own cellulitis screen negative (no ascending hot spreading erythema, no red streaks, no purulence, no regional lymphadenopathy), so this is neither an emergency (not T4/T5) nor an urgent same-day problem (not T3). It is telehealth/GP-manageable with strict drying, a topical antifungal, symptom relief and footwear/decontamination advice. Because the open, weeping fissure is a genuine portal of entry for secondary bacterial cellulitis AND telehealth cannot palpate the foot to exclude early deep infection, the disposition is PROMPT in-person GP review (T2, within 24-72 hours) rather than pure self-care (T0/T1) — and prominent mandatory escalation edges are attached for secondary bacterial cellulitis/sepsis. A systemically-well immunocompetent patient reviewed routinely by ~7 days with drying, a topical antifungal and an explicit cellulitis safety-net also passes (minimum viable T1). Reflexive escalation to 000/ED here is over-triage.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The top of the foot becomes bright red, hot and swollen, red streaks travel up the foot or leg, thick or foul-smelling pus appears, or fever/uncontrollable shivering develops
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day in-person / emergency assessment if any of these develop
escalation_edges.rationale: Indicates a secondary bacterial cellulitis entering through the fissure portal that needs systemic antibiotics and in-person (possibly hospital) assessment — a complication telehealth cannot palpate for.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the top of your foot becomes bright red, hot and swollen, you see red streaks travelling up your foot or leg, thick smelly pus appears, or you get a fever or the shivers, present to an emergency department the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Pain far out of proportion to the visible crack, rapidly spreading dusky/darkening skin, skin breakdown, or feeling very unwell/faint/collapsing
escalation_edges.rationale: These are warning features of a necrotising soft-tissue infection or systemic sepsis — a surgical/medical emergency needing an ambulance and immediate hospital care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain becomes far worse than the crack looks, the skin rapidly darkens or breaks down, or you feel very unwell, faint or feverish and shivery, call 000 straight away and tell them you have a spreading foot infection.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The fissure has not started to close or the pain/weeping is not improving after a few days of treatment, or the scaling is spreading
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Prompt GP review within 24-48 hours if not improving
escalation_edges.rationale: A fissure that fails to settle may be misdiagnosed, secondarily infected, or reflect an inadequately treated dermatophyte reservoir needing in-person review and possibly a skin scraping or a change of treatment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the crack hasn't started to close or the pain and weeping aren't easing within a few days, book a prompt GP review to check the skin and the treatment.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Baseline: severe but systemically-well interdigital tinea pedis with an open fissure, cellulitis screen negative
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Start drying + topical antifungal now and arrange prompt in-person GP review within 24-72 hours (by ~7 days at the latest)
escalation_edges.rationale: The proportionate disposition once emergencies are excluded — begin patient-performable treatment immediately and be seen in person soon because the open broken skin cannot be safely assessed for early infection over telehealth.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Start keeping the toes dry and using the antifungal cream now, and arrange to be seen in person by your GP within the next day or two so they can check the crack and make sure no infection is setting in.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Open, weeping interdigital fissure (broken skin barrier / portal of entry)
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold for prompt in-person review and for acting on the secondary-infection triggers — the open skin is why this is not pure self-care despite being systemically well.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Ongoing occupational exposure — heavy occlusive work boots and hyperhidrosis during back-to-back shifts
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Reinforce footwear/sock/decontamination measures and, where possible, off-loading and airing the foot while the fissure heals; continued occlusion will delay healing and drive recurrence.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a common fungal skin infection (athlete's foot) that has cracked the skin between your toes — treatable and not an emergency. Right now, gently cleanse and pat the toes completely dry (a cool hair-dryer helps the web spaces), keep the foot out of the sweaty boot and elevated, use the combined antifungal/anti-inflammatory cream on the painful crack and an antifungal cream over the whole sole and web spaces. Because the skin is open and broken, arrange to be seen in person by your GP within the next day or two (and by about a week at the latest) to check the crack. Do NOT use a strong steroid cream on its own, and you don't need antibiotics unless an infection develops.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Book a prompt in-person GP appointment (within 24-72 hours); if after hours use an after-hours GP or 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) for advice
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If this is after hours you do not need an emergency department for the crack itself — start the drying and antifungal measures and see your GP in the next day or two. Only seek same-day/emergency care if a red-flag develops: the top of the foot going bright red, hot and swollen, red streaks up the foot or leg, thick foul pus, or fever/rigors (call 000 if you feel very unwell or the skin is rapidly darkening/breaking down).
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Tell the GP the itchy flaky skin between the toes has been present for a couple of years and worsened over the last month with occlusive work boots and sweating, that a deep painful fissure opened today with serous weeping and malodour but no fever or ascending redness, and note the topical antifungal/steroid regimen started; mention any home temperature readings.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether there is early secondary bacterial cellulitis entering through the open fissure — telehealth cannot palpate for heat, induration or tenderness beyond the visible crack or feel for inguinal lymphadenopathy.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the uncertainty as a reason for prompt in-person review plus explicit secondary-infection safety-netting, while starting patient-performable antifungal/drying measures now — NOT as a reason to default to 000/ED when the patient is systemically well.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either dismissing the open broken-skin fissure with pure self-care and no follow-up (under-action), or escalating a systemically-well patient with a negative cellulitis screen straight to the emergency department / 000 (over-escalation).

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the presentation as trivial with pure self-care and no clinician review or safety-net, in a patient with an open, weeping, broken-skin fissure that is a portal of entry for cellulitis.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An unrecognised early secondary cellulitis progresses through the fissure to systemic sepsis before anyone lays eyes on the foot or advises when to escalate.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Advising only a routine (1-2 week) GP appointment with NO secondary-infection safety-net for an open fissure — acceptable only when an explicit cellulitis safety-net is attached; without it, review of the broken skin is unduly delayed.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a systemically-well patient with a negative cellulitis screen to urgent same-day care, the emergency department, or calling 000 for a common interdigital tinea pedis — unnecessary ED load and patient distress/cost when prompt in-person GP review is proportionate.
